sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his 10-year-old son.
He landed awkwardly and twisted his ankle, resulting in pain, swelling, and instability on the outside of the ankle.
He has been having trouble walking and feels that the ankle does not feel solid or stable.
He recently got back into working out and does not want to disrupt his progress.
He also mentioned that he is a volunteer coach starting in April and is concerned about the impact on his coaching responsibilities.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has exquisite tenderness on the lateral aspect of the ankle, with some tenderness on the medial deltoid region.
There is swelling on the lateral side of the ankle, but no epidermolysis, and the skin is intact.
Capillary refill is brisk, and there is no malalignment.
The patient has trouble walking due to instability and pain.
Test Results:
X-ray of the right ankle: A displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a lateral malleolar fracture, which is a displaced fracture on the lateral side of the ankle that is causing instability and pain.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; status: confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 7.5mg PO BID for pain management and swelling reduction.
I recommended icing and elevating the ankle regularly.
I advised the patient to use crutches and avoid putting weight on the affected ankle.
I also told the patient to continue coaching his son's team, as he will likely be out for about three months.
We will follow up in three months to reassess the fracture.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in three months to reassess the fracture and plan for further treatment.
